summary: The 987 Cayman shares the 997's IMS bearing concern on early models but is otherwise a robust sports car. The mid-engine layout is mechanically simpler than the 911.

issues.name: IMS Bearing Failure (987.1)
issues.description: The intermediate shaft bearing can fail on 987.1 models (2006–2008), potentially destroying the engine. The 987.2 (2009+) with DFI engine eliminated this risk.

issues.name: Bore Scoring
issues.description: Cylinder bore scoring from failed Nikasil or Lokasil coatings can cause oil consumption and power loss. More common on the 3.4L S models.

issues.name: RMS (Rear Main Seal) Leak
issues.description: Oil leak from the rear main seal between engine and transmission. Less labor-intensive on the Cayman than the 911 due to the mid-engine layout.

issues.name: AOS (Air-Oil Separator) Failure
issues.description: The air-oil separator fails and causes rough idle, oil consumption, and white smoke from the exhaust. A common flat-6 issue.

issues.name: Convertible Top Motor (Boxster)
issues.description: While this is a Cayman (hardtop), some buyers cross-shop with the Boxster. Boxster top motors and hydraulics are a known failure point.

buyingTips: 987.2 (2009+) is the smart buy — eliminates the IMS bearing concern entirely with the DFI engine
Get a bore scope inspection before buying any 987 — bore scoring is expensive and undetectable without inspection
IMS bearing retrofit on 987.1 cars is a worthwhile preventive measure — LN Engineering single-row bearing is the standard
Check for oil leaks under the car — the RMS and AOS are common sources
The Cayman S (3.4L) is the more desirable engine but slightly more prone to bore scoring than the base 2.7L
